subject: Pediatrics
stem: A mother brings her 3-month-old infant for a well-child visit. The pediatrician assesses gross motor development. Which of the following is the EXPECTED gross motor milestone at this age?
options: Bears weight on legs when held standing
explanation: At 3 months, the expected gross motor milestone is head control in the sitting position (when pulled to sit, the infant can hold the head in line with the body). Rolling prone to supine occurs at 4 months, sitting without support at 8 months, and bearing weight on legs at 7 months.
highYield: 3 months = head control in sitting; this is a classic NEET PG favorite. 'Head lag disappears' is the clinical correlate tested frequently.
pyqNote: Asked in NEET PG 2019 and AIIMS 2016 in similar format testing 3-month milestones.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 6-month-old infant is brought for vaccination. On developmental assessment, which gross motor milestone should the infant have achieved?
explanation: At 6 months, an infant should be able to sit with support. Sitting without support is achieved at 8 months. Standing holding furniture occurs at 9 months. Rolling both ways (not just prone to supine) is achieved by 5 months.
highYield: 6 months = sits WITH support. 8 months = sits WITHOUT support. This distinction is heavily tested in NEET PG.
pyqNote: Classic NEET PG question — the distinction between 'sits with support' (6 months) vs 'sits without support' (8 months) is a perennial trap.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 10-month-old infant is assessed for developmental progress. The mother reports the child is moving along furniture while holding on to it. Which term BEST describes this motor activity?
explanation: Cruising refers to walking while holding on to furniture, which is the expected gross motor milestone at 10 months. Crawling (belly on ground) occurs around 7-8 months, creeping (on hands and knees) around 8-9 months. Bear walking (on hands and feet) is not a standard milestone.
highYield: Cruising = walks holding furniture = 10 months. This specific terminology is tested in NEET PG. 11 months = stands alone; 12-13 months = walks alone (mean 13 months).
pyqNote: The term 'cruising' and its age (10 months) has appeared in NEET PG and DNB pattern questions.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A child is observed to hop on one foot during a developmental assessment. This milestone is expected to be achieved at which age?
explanation: Hopping on one foot is expected at 4 years. At 3 years, a child can walk upstairs with alternating feet. At 5 years, a child can skip. At 6 years, most children can ride a bicycle.
highYield: Memory aid: 3 years = alternating feet upstairs; 4 years = hops on one foot; 5 years = skips. This sequence is tested frequently.
pyqNote: Hopping on one foot at 4 years is a high-yield NEET PG milestone.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A mother is concerned that her 18-month-old child cannot yet run. During examination, the child walks well but does not run. The pediatrician notes that the child's development is:
options: Normal, as running is expected at 24 months
Delayed, as running should be present by 18 months
Normal, as walking well by 18 months is the expected milestone
Delayed, as running should be present by 15 months
explanation: Running is expected at 18 months. Walking well is achieved at 15 months. At 18 months, expected milestones include running (in addition to walking well). This child who can walk well but cannot run at 18 months has a delay in the running milestone.
highYield: 15 months = walks well; 18 months = runs. At 24 months = goes up stairs one step at a time and kicks a ball. These ages are critical for NEET PG.
pyqNote: The 15-month vs 18-month distinction (walks well vs runs) is a NEET PG trap question.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 9-month-old infant is observed picking up small objects during a developmental assessment. The MOST advanced grasp pattern expected at this age is:
explanation: Pincer grasp (using tip of thumb and index finger) develops at 9-10 months. Thumb-finger grasp (inferior pincer) appears at 9 months. Raking grasp uses all four fingers together and appears at 7 months. Palmar grasp is present from birth as a reflex but voluntary palmar grasp occurs around 5-6 months.
highYield: Raking grasp = 7 months; Thumb-finger grasp = 9 months; Pincer grasp = 9-10 months. These are frequently tested in sequence.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2017 tested the age of pincer grasp development. The progression from raking to pincer is high yield.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A child can build a tower of 6 cubes during a developmental assessment. This fine motor skill is expected to be achieved at:
explanation: Building a tower of 6 cubes is a milestone achieved at 24 months (2 years). Tower of 2 cubes is achieved at 15 months. Tower of 3-4 cubes at 18 months. Tower of 8-9 cubes at 3 years. Tower of 10 cubes at 3.5 years.
highYield: Tower milestones: 15 months = 2 cubes; 18 months = 3-4 cubes; 24 months = 6 cubes; 3 years = 8-9 cubes. Each cube = approximately 3 months of age after 15 months.
pyqNote: Tower of cubes is one of the most frequently tested fine motor milestones in NEET PG and AIIMS.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 3-year-old child is assessed for fine motor skills. Which geometric shape should the child be able to COPY at this age?
explanation: At 3 years, a child can copy a circle. Copying a cross (+) is achieved at 4 years. Copying a triangle is achieved at 5 years. Copying a diamond/square is achieved at 6-7 years. Memory aid: 3=circle, 4=cross, 5=triangle.
highYield: Shape copying sequence: 3 years=circle, 4 years=cross, 5 years=triangle. This is one of the most tested fine motor sequences in NEET PG.
pyqNote: Asked in multiple NEET PG papers. The sequence circle-cross-triangle at ages 3-4-5 years is a classic.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 15-month-old child is brought for developmental assessment. Which of the following combinations of fine motor milestones is EXPECTED at this age?
options: Scribbles spontaneously and builds tower of 2 cubes
Copies circle and builds tower of 4 cubes
Points to wants and uses pincer grasp
explanation: At 15 months, expected fine motor milestones include: scribbles spontaneously and builds a tower of 2 cubes. Pointing to wants is a 12-month milestone. Pincer grasp is 9-10 months. Copying a circle is a 3-year milestone. Transferring hand to hand is a 6-month milestone.
highYield: 15 months key milestones: scribbles + tower of 2 cubes (fine motor); 2-3 words (language); walks well (gross motor). This combined milestone testing is NEET PG pattern.
pyqNote: Combined milestone questions at 15 months are commonly asked to test comprehensive developmental knowledge.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: At what age does a normal infant begin to babble (producing consonant-vowel combinations like 'ba', 'da', 'ga')?
explanation: Babbling (consonant-vowel combinations) begins at 6 months. At 2 months, an infant coos (soft vowel sounds). At 4 months, an infant squeals. At 8 months, the infant says 'mama/dada' non-specifically.
highYield: Language progression: 2 months=coos; 4 months=squeals; 6 months=babbles; 8 months=mama/dada non-specific; 10 months=mama/dada specific. This sequence is essential for NEET PG.
pyqNote: Babbling at 6 months is a very frequently tested NEET PG question. Distinguish from cooing (2 months) and squealing (4 months).

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A mother reports that her child says 'mama' and 'dada' but uses these words to refer to both parents interchangeably and not exclusively to either parent. At what age is this behavior NORMALLY expected?
explanation: Non-specific use of 'mama' and 'dada' (not directed to the appropriate parent) is expected at 8 months. By 10 months, the child uses 'mama' and 'dada' specifically to refer to the correct parent. At 12 months, the child says 1 word other than mama/dada.
highYield: 8 months = mama/dada non-specific; 10 months = mama/dada specific. This distinction is frequently tested. The word 'specific' is the key differentiator.
pyqNote: The specific vs non-specific use of mama/dada is a classic NEET PG trap tested in multiple papers.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 2-year-old child is brought for evaluation. Which of the following language milestones should the child have ALREADY achieved by this age?
explanation: Two-word phrases are expected at 18-24 months, so by 2 years this should be achieved. Giving full name is a 3-year milestone. Naming 4 colors is a 4-year milestone. Three-word sentences are expected at 2-3 years (so at exactly 2 years, this may not yet be established).
highYield: 18-24 months = 2-word phrases; 2 years = names body parts; 3 years = full name + 3-word sentences; 4 years = names 4 colors.
pyqNote: Language milestones at 2 years are high yield. 2-word phrases by 24 months is also a RED FLAG if absent.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A parent reports their 18-month-old child uses only 5 words. According to standard developmental milestones, the minimum expected vocabulary at 18 months is:
explanation: At 18 months, a child is expected to have a vocabulary of 10 or more words. A child with only 5 words at 18 months has a language delay. At 15 months, 2-3 words are expected. At 12 months, 1 word (other than mama/dada) is expected.
highYield: 12 months = 1 word; 15 months = 2-3 words; 18 months = 10+ words; 24 months = 50+ words and 2-word phrases. The '10+ words at 18 months' criterion is high yield.
pyqNote: Vocabulary size at 18 months (10 words minimum) is a NEET PG favorite and is also the threshold for diagnosing language delay.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A mother reports that her infant started smiling in response to her face and voice. At what age does social smile NORMALLY first appear?
explanation: Social smile (a smile in response to a human face or voice) normally appears at 6 weeks (1.5 months). A smile at 4 weeks may be a 'gas smile' or reflexive smile. By 3 months, the infant smiles broadly in social interactions, but the FIRST appearance of social smile is at 6 weeks.
highYield: Social smile = 6 weeks = 1.5 months. This is one of the most commonly tested milestones in NEET PG. Distinguished from the reflexive neonatal smile.
pyqNote: Social smile at 6 weeks is asked in nearly every NEET PG paper pattern. Also a key red flag: no social smile by 3 months.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 13-month-old child has not spoken a single word other than 'mama' and 'dada'. The parents are worried. According to ALARM signs of developmental delay, which of the following statements is CORRECT?
options: This is normal; single words appear at 15 months
This is a red flag; no single word by 12 months is an ALARM sign
This is a red flag; single words should appear by 10 months
This is borderline; reassess at 18 months
explanation: According to ALARM (Autistic red flags and Language milestone) signs, absence of a single meaningful word (other than mama/dada) by 12 months is a developmental red flag. The child in question is 13 months with no word — this is a red flag requiring further evaluation. The red flag criterion is 'no single word by 16 months' in some guidelines, but 'no single word by 12 months' is an absolute concern in Indian PG exam contexts.
highYield: ALARM red flags: no social smile by 3 months; no babbling by 12 months; no single word by 12-16 months; no 2-word phrases by 24 months; ANY regression. These are critical for NEET PG.
pyqNote: ALARM signs are asked directly in NEET PG and DNB. Memorize all 5 red flag criteria.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: Which of the following findings in a 24-month-old child is the MOST CONCERNING red flag for autism spectrum disorder?
explanation: No 2-word spontaneous phrases by 24 months is an ALARM sign for autism spectrum disorder and language delay. Hopping on one foot is a 4-year milestone (not expected at 24 months). Copying a circle is a 3-year milestone (not expected at 24 months). Naming 4 colors is a 4-year milestone. The only item that represents a developmental red flag at 24 months is the absence of 2-word phrases.
highYield: No 2-word phrase by 24 months = absolute ALARM sign. This is the most important language red flag and is directly linked to ASD screening.
pyqNote: Red flag questions combining normal milestone knowledge with ASD screening criteria are high yield in recent NEET PG papers.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 6-week-old infant is brought for evaluation. The pediatrician assesses vision. Which visual behavior is expected at this age?
options: Fixes and follows a face or light
explanation: At 6 weeks, an infant fixes on and follows a face or light. Reaching for objects is a 4-month milestone. Tracking through 90 degrees is a 2-month milestone. Stranger anxiety is a social milestone occurring at 6-8 months.
highYield: Vision milestones: 6 weeks = fixes and follows; 2 months = tracks through 90 degrees; 4 months = reaches for objects. These are frequently tested in NEET PG.
pyqNote: Visual fixing and following at 6 weeks is closely tied to the social smile milestone, making 6-week milestones a combined favorite topic.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 12-month-old infant is brought for assessment. Which of the following COMBINATIONS of milestones is EXPECTED at this age?
options: Walks alone, says 5 words, builds tower of 2 cubes
Stands alone, says 1 word, points to objects, uses pincer grasp
Walks holding furniture, babbles, raking grasp
Sits without support, mama/dada specific, reaches for objects
explanation: At 12 months: gross motor = stands alone (11 months) and walks with support/beginning to walk; fine motor = pincer grasp (9-10 months); language = 1 meaningful word (other than mama/dada), points to communicate; social = points to wants. Walking alone is 12-13 months (mean 13 months), so only beginning at 12 months. Tower of 2 cubes is 15 months. Babbling is 6 months. Raking grasp is 7 months.
highYield: 12-month milestone package: stands alone (11 months achieved), 1 word other than mama/dada, points, pincer grasp. This composite is very frequently tested.
pyqNote: Combined milestone questions at 12 months are among the most frequently asked in NEET PG pediatrics section.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A child can walk upstairs placing both feet on each step (not alternating). At what age is this milestone EXPECTED?
explanation: Walking upstairs placing both feet on each step (one step at a time) is a 24-month milestone. Alternating feet going upstairs is achieved at 3 years. Skipping is a 5-year milestone. At 18 months, a child runs but does not yet climb stairs in this coordinated manner.
highYield: Stair climbing: 24 months = one step at a time (both feet on each step); 3 years = alternating feet going up; 4 years = alternating feet going down.
pyqNote: Stair climbing milestones with the specific technique (both feet vs alternating) is a classic NEET PG question.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 4-year-old child is asked to copy a shape drawn by the examiner. Which geometric shape is the child EXPECTED to be able to copy at this age?
explanation: A 4-year-old child can copy both a circle (achieved at 3 years) and a cross (achieved at 4 years). A triangle is copied at 5 years. A diamond/rhombus is copied at 6-7 years. Since the question asks what a 4-year-old CAN copy, the answer includes all shapes up to their age level — circle AND cross.
highYield: By age 4, child can copy circle (3-year skill) and cross (4-year skill). The question often asks 'newest' milestone — for 4 years that is copying a cross.
pyqNote: Shape-copying sequence is asked in almost every NEET PG paper. Ensure you know: 3=circle, 4=cross, 5=triangle.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 4-year-old child is assessed for language development. Which of the following language abilities is expected at this age?
explanation: Naming 4 colors is a 4-year language milestone. Giving first and last name (full name) is a 3-year milestone. Using 3-word sentences is a 2-3 year milestone. Using 2-word phrases is an 18-24 month milestone.
highYield: 4-year language milestones: names 4 colors; asks 'why' questions; tells stories. Giving full name is 3 years. These are frequently confused in NEET PG questions.
pyqNote: Naming colors at 4 years vs giving full name at 3 years is a classic NEET PG confusion trap.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A mother reports her 4-month-old infant just started doing something new. When placed on the tummy, the baby lifts its head to 90 degrees. At what age should head raising to 45 degrees in the prone position FIRST appear?
explanation: Head raising to 45 degrees in the prone position first appears at 2 months. At 1 month, the infant can lift the head momentarily in the prone position (briefly). Head control of 90 degrees in prone occurs at 4 months. Full head control in sitting occurs at 3 months.
highYield: Prone head control progression: 1 month = lifts head momentarily; 2 months = head 45 degrees prone; 4 months = head 90 degrees prone. Frequently tested as a sequence.
pyqNote: The sequence of prone head control is tested in NEET PG pattern questions, especially distinguishing 45 degrees (2 months) from 90 degrees (4 months).

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 5-month-old infant is assessed. The mother reports the baby can now roll both ways. This milestone correlates with which age of acquisition?
explanation: Rolling in both directions (prone to supine AND supine to prone) is expected at 5 months. Rolling from prone to supine only occurs at 4 months. This infant is achieving the 5-month milestone appropriately.
highYield: Rolling milestones: 4 months = prone to supine; 5 months = both ways (prone to supine AND supine to prone). The 'both ways' criterion distinguishes 5 months from 4 months.
pyqNote: The distinction between rolling one way (4 months) versus both ways (5 months) is a classic NEET PG exam trap.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 4-month-old infant reaches out toward a colorful toy held in front of her. This fine motor/visual milestone is expected at which age?
explanation: Reaching for objects is expected at 4 months. This is also the age of 'voluntary grasp' beginning and is a key fine motor milestone. At 2 months, the infant tracks through 90 degrees. At 6 months, the infant transfers objects from hand to hand.
highYield: 4 months = reaches for objects (fine motor) + head 90 degrees prone (gross motor) + squeals (language). This combination at 4 months is frequently tested.
pyqNote: Reaching for objects at 4 months is tested both as a fine motor AND as a visual milestone in NEET PG.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A mother reports that her infant started crying and becoming anxious whenever strangers approach or hold the baby. This is a normal developmental phenomenon. At what age does stranger anxiety FIRST appear?
explanation: Stranger anxiety normally first appears at 6-8 months and peaks at around 8-12 months. It reflects the infant's developing ability to differentiate familiar from unfamiliar faces and is a sign of healthy attachment and social-cognitive development.
highYield: Stranger anxiety = 6-8 months. This is a social milestone, not a gross motor or language milestone. It indicates normal cognitive and social development.
pyqNote: Stranger anxiety timing (6-8 months) is asked in NEET PG. It is sometimes confused with separation anxiety (peaks 10-18 months).

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 6-month-old infant is observed passing a toy from one hand to the other during examination. This milestone is called:
explanation: Hand-to-hand transfer (transferring objects from one hand to the other) is a fine motor milestone achieved at 6 months. Raking grasp appears at 7 months. Thumb-finger grasp at 9 months. Pincer grasp at 9-10 months.
highYield: 6 months fine motor milestone = hand-to-hand transfer. This follows the sequence: reaching for objects (4 months) → transfer hand to hand (6 months) → raking grasp (7 months).
pyqNote: Hand-to-hand transfer at 6 months is a commonly tested fine motor milestone in NEET PG.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 3-year-old child can give his full name when asked. He uses sentences like 'I want water' (3-word sentences). However, he cannot name any colors. His developmental profile is:
options: Normal for his age in all domains
Advanced in language; naming colors is a 5-year skill
Normal; naming 4 colors is a 4-year skill
Delayed; he should be naming colors by 3 years
explanation: Giving full name (3-year skill), using 3-word sentences (2-3 year skill) — this child's language is appropriate for 3 years. Naming 4 colors is a 4-year milestone. Therefore, not naming colors at 3 years is NORMAL. The child is developing appropriately.
highYield: 3-year language milestones: gives full name, 3-word sentences, knows age and gender. 4-year milestone: names 4 colors. This distinction is a classic NEET PG trap.
pyqNote: The 3-year vs 4-year language milestone distinction (full name vs naming colors) appears frequently in NEET PG.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: At what age does a normal child FIRST walk independently without support?
explanation: Independent walking without support begins at 12-13 months, with a mean age of 13 months. At 11 months, the child stands alone. At 10 months, the child cruises (walks holding furniture). Walking WELL (mature gait) is achieved at 15 months.
highYield: The critical distinction: walks alone = 12-13 months (mean 13); walks WELL = 15 months. Many students confuse 'begins to walk' (12-13 months) with 'walks well' (15 months).
pyqNote: Onset of walking is one of the most asked NEET PG questions. The mean age of 13 months is specifically tested.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 2-month-old infant makes soft, vowel-like sounds ('oo', 'aa', 'ee') when the mother talks to her. This vocalization is called:
explanation: Cooing (soft, vowel-like sounds) begins at 2 months and is the earliest form of social vocalization. Squealing begins at 4 months. Babbling (consonant-vowel combinations) begins at 6 months. Jargon (strings of babble with sentence-like intonation) appears around 9-12 months.
highYield: Language timeline: 2 months = coos; 4 months = squeals; 6 months = babbles; 9-12 months = jargon/mama-dada non-specific. Cooing at 2 months is the starting point.
pyqNote: The term 'cooing' and its age (2 months) is directly asked in NEET PG and DNB papers.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 9-month-old infant was previously babbling 'ba-ba-da-da' but the mother reports the child has stopped vocalizing over the past 2 months. There is no family history of hearing loss. What is the MOST appropriate interpretation?
options: Normal variation; babbling may pause temporarily
Developmental regression — an absolute ALARM sign requiring urgent evaluation
The child is focusing on motor development instead
Reassess at 12 months if no words appear
explanation: Any regression or loss of previously acquired milestones is an absolute red flag (ALARM sign) in developmental pediatrics. Loss of babbling, pointing, language, or social skills may indicate autism spectrum disorder, a neurological disorder, or hearing loss. This must NEVER be dismissed as normal variation and requires urgent comprehensive evaluation.
highYield: REGRESSION OF ANY MILESTONE = absolute ALARM sign. This is the most important concept in developmental pediatrics. Loss of language is a key early sign of ASD.
pyqNote: Regression/loss of milestones is tested in NEET PG scenario-based questions and is the #1 red flag that must never be missed.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 7-month-old infant picks up food from the tray by raking it toward the palm using all four fingers together without using the thumb. This grasp is called raking grasp. At what age does the MORE MATURE thumb-finger grasp REPLACE this pattern?
explanation: The thumb-finger grasp (an inferior pincer, using the thumb and index finger but not yet at the fingertips) replaces the raking grasp at 9 months. The mature pincer grasp (using the very tips of the thumb and index finger) is refined at 9-10 months. Raking grasp appears at 7 months.
highYield: Grasp progression: palmar reflex (birth) → voluntary palmar grasp (5-6 months) → raking grasp (7 months) → thumb-finger grasp (9 months) → pincer grasp (9-10 months). This sequence is critical for NEET PG.
pyqNote: The grasp progression sequence is a classic NEET PG question. Distinguishing raking (7 months) from thumb-finger (9 months) from pincer (9-10 months) is essential.
